audienceSections.normal: In Minji and Junho meeting politely, you will use [[grammar:N은/는 — topic marking]]. Put [[grammar:은]] after a consonant sound and [[grammar:는]] after a vowel sound to mark the topic. 저는 is 저 + 는. Structural gloss: ‘as for me.’ Natural English: ‘I.’ Do not translate 은/는 as a separate English word every time.
audienceSections.expert: This chapter presents [[grammar:N은/는 — topic marking]] in Minji and Junho meeting politely. 은/는 marks a discourse topic in topic–comment structure, with phonologically conditioned allomorphy. 저는 decomposes as 저 + 는; the copula separately alternates between 이에요 and 예요.

audienceSections.normal: Put [[grammar:은]] after a consonant sound and [[grammar:는]] after a vowel sound to mark the topic. 저는 is 저 + 는. Structural gloss: ‘as for me.’ Natural English: ‘I.’ Do not translate 은/는 as a separate English word every time.
audienceSections.expert: 은/는 marks a discourse topic in topic–comment structure, with phonologically conditioned allomorphy. 저는 decomposes as 저 + 는; the copula separately alternates between 이에요 and 예요.

audienceSections.normal: Read each Hangul block as one syllable. Particles stay attached to the word before them, but the breakdown explains what each particle does.
audienceSections.expert: The examples use standard Korean in the informal-polite 해요 style. Particle allomorphy and contractions are analyzed as grammar while lexical entries retain citation forms.

breakdown.normal:
Read each exact korean line, then check its natural English meaning. Watch for [[grammar:N은/는 — topic marking]] where it appears.

- 안녕하세요. — Hello.
- 안녕하세요. — Hello.
- 저는 민지예요. — I am Minji.
- 저는 준호예요. — I am Junho.
- 학생이에요. — I am a student.
- 네, 저는 선생님이에요. — Yes, I am a teacher.
breakdown.expert:
These exact reading lines provide the distributional evidence for [[grammar:N은/는 — topic marking]]. The grammar section above analyzes the relevant morphology and syntax.

- 안녕하세요. — Hello.
